description: Fix the broken SQL query so it executes correctly and returns the expected results.

scenarios.description: Fix missing GROUP BY clause in aggregation query
scenarios.schema_sql: CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL, status TEXT); CREATE TABLE customers (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, email TEXT);
scenarios.data_sql: INSERT INTO customers VALUES (1,'Alice','alice@example.com'),(2,'Bob','bob@example.com'),(3,'Carol','carol@example.com'); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (1,1,150.0,'paid'),(2,1,200.0,'paid'),(3,2,75.0,'pending'),(4,3,300.0,'paid'),(5,2,125.0,'paid');
scenarios.broken_query: SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) FROM orders WHERE status = 'paid'
scenarios.fixed_query_example: SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) FROM orders WHERE status = 'paid' GROUP BY customer_id
scenarios.hint: Aggregation functions like SUM require a GROUP BY clause when combined with non-aggregated columns.

scenarios.description: Fix wrong JOIN condition causing cartesian product
scenarios.schema_sql: CREATE TABLE products (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, category_id INTEGER, price REAL); CREATE TABLE categories (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT);
scenarios.data_sql: INSERT INTO categories VALUES (1,'Electronics'),(2,'Clothing'),(3,'Books'); INSERT INTO products VALUES (1,'Laptop',1,999.0),(2,'T-Shirt',2,29.0),(3,'Python Book',3,49.0),(4,'Headphones',1,199.0);
scenarios.broken_query: SELECT p.name, c.name FROM products p JOIN categories c ON p.id = c.id
scenarios.fixed_query_example: SELECT p.name, c.name FROM products p JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.id
scenarios.hint: The JOIN condition should link the foreign key to the primary key.

scenarios.description: Fix syntax error and incorrect WHERE clause
scenarios.schema_sql: CREATE TABLE employees (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, department TEXT, salary REAL, hire_date TEXT);
scenarios.broken_query: SELECT name, salary FROM employees WHERE department = 'Engineering' AND salary > 90000
scenarios.fixed_query_example: SELECT name, salary FROM employees WHERE department = 'Engineering' AND salary > 90000
scenarios.hint: Check all parentheses are balanced and string literals use single quotes.

scenarios.schema_sql: CREATE TABLE sales (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, product TEXT, region TEXT, amount REAL, sale_date TEXT);
scenarios.broken_query: SELECT product, SUM(amount) as total FROM sales HAVING SUM(amount) > 800
scenarios.fixed_query_example: SELECT product, SUM(amount) as total FROM sales GROUP BY product HAVING SUM(amount) > 800
scenarios.hint: HAVING filters groups, you need GROUP BY before HAVING.

scenarios.description: Fix ambiguous column name in JOIN query
scenarios.schema_sql: CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, created_at TEXT); CREATE TABLE posts (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, user_id INTEGER, title TEXT, created_at TEXT);
scenarios.data_sql: INSERT INTO users VALUES (1,'Alice','2023-01-01'),(2,'Bob','2023-02-01'); INSERT INTO posts VALUES (1,1,'First Post','2024-01-01'),(2,1,'Second Post','2024-02-01'),(3,2,'Hello World','2024-01-15');
scenarios.broken_query: SELECT name, title, created_at FROM users u JOIN posts p ON u.id = p.user_id
scenarios.fixed_query_example: SELECT u.name, p.title, p.created_at FROM users u JOIN posts p ON u.id = p.user_id
scenarios.hint: When multiple tables have columns with the same name, qualify with table alias.
